description: A potter changes the curve of clay, a carver works a figure from wood, or a pencil gives a sheep its drawn outline. 'kire' gives material or a line a particular configuration. 'norelu' (form) concerns a definite structure coming together, while 'theko' (craft) adds practiced skill to the making.
articulatory_notes: Press the back of the tongue against the soft palate for unvoiced 'k' and release into stressed high-front unrounded 'i'. Tap the tongue tip against the alveolar ridge, then finish with middle-height front unrounded 'e'.
sound_symbolism: A firm velar release and quick tap make 'kire' concise in the mouth. It feels like the small decisive adjustment by which a line becomes recognisable.
usage_notes: The material or drawn subject is the direct object. A tool takes instrumental 'roe', and a resulting configuration may be stated with 'muo' (INTO). In noun position, 'kire' names the act or work of shaping and drawing, as well as shape or form.

pillars.art-nouveau-aesthetics: Art Nouveau shaping often followed the curve already visible in a stem, body, or length of worked material. 'kire' can name both the observed contour and the hand's answer to it.

semantic_domains.activity: working material or a line into a chosen configuration
semantic_domains.creation: giving an object or image its outline and surface form
